Assignment 1: PyTorch Basics¶

Overview¶

In this assignment, you will:

  1. Build a simple neural network from scratch to understand how neural networks work.
  2. Train a neural network (using PyTorch) to classify images from the Garbage Classification dataset into one of 7 classes (battery, biological, cardboard, etc.).

The provided code will guide you through key steps, but by the end of the assignment, you should:

  1. Understand the training loop for a machine learning model.
  2. Distinguish between training, validation, and test data.
  3. Learn about overfitting and underfitting.
  4. Explore how hyperparameters, like learning rate and batch size, impact training.
  5. Compare a basic feedforward neural network (ANN) with a convolutional neural network (CNN).

This assignment is inspired by materials developed by Prof. Lisa Zhang.

What to Submit¶

  1. Submit the .ipynb and .html files containing your code, outputs, and answers from all parts. Please take extra effort to make your answers and submissions readable. Do not display unnecessary outputs, only the ones that are important for supporting your answers.

You can produce a HTML file directly from Google Colab. The Colab instructions are provided at the end of this document.

  1. Submit your model predictions on the secret test dataset for Parts B-5 and B-6 as labels_part_b5.csv and labels_part_b6.csv.

Important: Do not submit additional files generated by your code.

Please use Google Colab for this assignment. If you prefer Jupyter Notebook, ensure the file is uploaded to Colab for submission.

Colab Link¶

Include a link to your colab file here and ensure the file can be accessed by the our teaching team.

Colab Link:

In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

https://colab.research.google.com/drive/11bI5nmgZFFS_w28CnrQZC-9SPHg2jA-K#scrollTo=W_HlhE6r8Rle

PART A: Building a Neural Network from Scratch [8pt]¶

Before we get into using PyTorch to train our classifier we will go through the process of creating our neural network from scratch. We've seen in the tutorial how to build a 1-layer network, now we'll take it one step further to build a 2-layer network. This is an important exercise that everyone should attempt at least once to understand and truly appreciate the workings of neural networks.

Helper Functions¶

To help guide the construction we will use the sklearn wine dataset. Provided are some helper code to get us started:

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.datasets import load_wine
import numpy as np

d = load_wine()
raw_data = np.c_[d.data, d.target]
raw_data = raw_data[~np.isnan(raw_data).any(axis=1)].astype(float)

np.random.shuffle(raw_data)
In [2]:
import numpy as np

# split your data into training and validation
X_train = raw_data[0:100,:13]
y_train = raw_data[0:100,13:14].astype(int)
X_val = raw_data[100:,:13]
y_val = raw_data[100:,13:14].astype(int)

print(X_train.shape, y_train.shape)
print(X_train.dtype, y_train.dtype)
print(X_val.shape, y_val.shape)
print(X_val.dtype, y_val.dtype)

(100, 13) (100, 1)
float64 int64
(78, 13) (78, 1)
float64 int64

Recall that the neural network output consists of several nodes, one for each output class. Since the labels are provided as integers we will need to convert them into one-hot vectors to match the neural network output format.

In [3]:
#Convert array to one-hot encoding
def to_one_hot(Y):
    n_col = np.amax(Y) + 1
    binarized = np.zeros((len(Y), n_col))
    for i in range(len(Y)):
        binarized[i, Y[i]] = 1.
    return binarized
In [4]:
y_train = to_one_hot(y_train)
print(X_train.shape, y_train.shape)
print(X_train.dtype, y_train.dtype)

y_val = to_one_hot(y_val)
print(X_val.shape, y_val.shape)
print(X_val.dtype, y_val.dtype)

(100, 13) (100, 3)
float64 float64
(78, 13) (78, 3)
float64 float64
In [5]:
#verify one-hot encoding
y_train[0:5,:]
Out[5]:
array([[0., 0., 1.],
       [1., 0., 0.],
       [0., 1., 0.],
       [0., 1., 0.],
       [1., 0., 0.]])

Part A-1. Develop a 2-layer ANN [6pt MODEL]¶

At its core a 2-layer neural network is just a few lines of code. Most of the complexity comes from setting up the training of the network.

Using vectorized form, set up the neural network training to use a cross-entropy loss function and determine the gradients with resepect to the layer 1 and layer 2 weights.

In [30]:
# write code to create a 2-layer ANN in vectorized form

#define sigmoid
def sigmoid(x):
    return 1/(1+np.exp(-x))

#define softmax
def softmax(x):
  e = np.exp(x)
  return e/e.sum(axis=1, keepdims = True)


def ann(W, X_train, y_train):

  num_hidden = 20
  num_features = 13
  num_outputs = 3

  #Weights
  w0 = W[:num_features*num_hidden].reshape(num_features, num_hidden)
  w1 = W[num_features*num_hidden:].reshape(num_hidden, num_outputs)

  #Feed forward
  layer0 = X_train
  layer1 = sigmoid(np.dot(layer0, w0))
  layer2 = np.dot(layer1, w1)

  # softmax
  y_pred = softmax(layer2)

  #Back propagation using gradient descent

  #cross-entropy loss
  error = -np.sum(y_train * np.log(y_pred + 1e-8)) / len(y_train) # TO BE COMPLETED

  #initialize gradients to zero
  dw0 = np.zeros_like(w0) # TO BE COMPLETED
  dw1 = np.zeros_like(w1) # TO BE COMPLETED

  #calculate gradients
  # TO BE COMPLETED
  delta2 = (y_pred - y_train) / X_train.shape[0]
  delta1 = delta2.dot(w1.T) * layer1 * (1 - layer1)

  #determine gradients
  dw1 += np.dot(layer1.T, delta2) # TO BE COMPLETED
  dw0 += np.dot(layer0.T, delta1) # TO BE COMPLETED

  #combine gradients into one vector
  dW = np.concatenate([dw0.reshape(-1), dw1.reshape(-1)]) # TO BE COMPLETED

  return (error, dW, y_pred)

Part A-2. Train your neural network [1pt RESULT]¶

Train your neural network once with random initialization (uniformly at random between -1 and +1), and once with zero initialization (all weights = 0).

Compare results. Comment on how well does your network work on the wine dataset in these two cases and why?

In [7]:
num_hidden = 20
num_features = 13
num_outputs = 3


def train_and_evaluate(w0_init, w1_init, label=""):

    #initial weights: w0_init, w1_init

    #combine weights into a single vector
    W = np.array(list(w0_init.flatten()) + list(w1_init.flatten()))

    #train network
    n = 0.001
    iterations = 100000
    errors = []
    for i in range(iterations):
        (error, dW, y_pred) = ann(W, X_train, y_train)
        W += -dW * n
        errors.append(error)

    # final predictions
    _, _, y_pred = ann(W, X_train, y_train)
    y_pred_labels = np.round(y_pred, 0)
    y_true_labels = np.round(y_train, 0)

    print(f"{label} initialization: Predictions on training data: \n{y_pred_labels[:5]}")
    print(f"{label} initialization: Ground truth training data: \n{y_true_labels[:5]}")

    accuracy = np.mean(np.argmax(y_pred, axis=1) == np.argmax(y_train, axis=1))
    print(f"{label} initialization: Final training accuracy: {accuracy:.4f}")

    return errors, accuracy
In [32]:
#random initialization
w0_rand = np.random.uniform(-1, 1, (num_features, num_hidden)) # TO BE COMPLETED
w1_rand = np.random.uniform(-1, 1, (num_hidden, num_outputs)) # TO BE COMPLETED
errors_rand, accuracy_rand = train_and_evaluate(w0_rand, w1_rand, "Random")

#zero initialization
w0_zero = np.zeros((num_features, num_hidden)) # TO BE COMPLETED
w1_zero = np.zeros((num_hidden, num_outputs)) # TO BE COMPLETED
errors_zero, acc_zero = train_and_evaluate(w0_zero, w1_zero, "Zero")

Zero initialization: Predictions on training data: 
[[0. 0. 0.]

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

The model perfromed well in the random initialization with final training accuracy of 71%,
while it did worse in zero initialization with training accuracy of 35%. The reason for this might be that in the zero initialization,
all the hidden neurons would have been computing the same output, while in the random initialization,
each neuron would have learned something different from the data.
Therefore, the gradient descent was successful and had a higher accuracy on the Wine dataset.




'''

Part A-3. Validation [2pt MODEL]¶

Validate that the gradients were computed correctly for the 2-layer neural network you developed.

In [ ]:
#write code to numerical verify the gradients you calculated

num_hidden = 20
num_features = 13
num_outputs = 3

#initialize weights uniformly at random between -1 and +1, and combine weights
w0 = np.random.uniform(-1, 1, (num_features, num_hidden)) # TO BE COMPLETED
w1 = np.random.uniform(-1, 1, (num_hidden, num_outputs))# TO BE COMPLETED
W = np.concatenate([w0.flatten(), w1.flatten()]) # TO BE COMPLETED

#compute gradients analytically
(error, dW, y_pred) = ann(W, X_train, y_train)

#compute gradients numerically
dW_num = np.zeros((len(W),1))

for ind in range(len(W)):
  #reset gradients
  We1 = np.array(list(w0.flatten()) + list(w1.flatten()))
  We2 = np.array(list(w0.flatten()) + list(w1.flatten()))

  #increment slightly
  We1[ind] = We1[ind] + 0.0001 # TO BE COMPLETED
  We2[ind] = We2[ind] - 0.0001 # TO BE COMPLETED

  #compute errors
  (error_e1, dW_e1, y_pred1) = ann(We1, X_train, y_train)
  (error_e2, dW_e2, y_pred2) = ann(We2, X_train, y_train)

  #obtain numerical gradients
  grad_num = (error_e1 - error_e2) / (2 * 0.0001) # TO BE COMPLETED

  #display difference between numerical and analytic gradients
  print(round(abs(grad_num - dW[ind]), 4), grad_num, dW[ind])

  return 1/(1+np.exp(-x))

PART B: Training with PyTorch¶

In the second part of the assignment we will see how we can use PyTorch to train a neural network to classify different garbage items.

In [34]:
import numpy as np
import time
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import torch.optim as optim
import torchvision
from torch.utils.data.sampler import SubsetRandomSampler
import os
from torchvision import datasets, transforms
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, random_split
import shutil

Part B-0. Helper Functions¶

We will be making use of the following helper functions. If you are using Google Colab, you may change runtime type to GPU to speed up the training process in this part. You will be asked to look at and possibly modify some of these, but you are not expected to understand all of them.

You should look at the function names and read the docstrings. If you are curious, come back and explore the code after making some progress on the lab.

In [ ]:
###############################################################################
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print("Using device:", device)

# Data Loading

def get_data_loader(data_dir, batch_size, valid_split=0.2):
    """
    Load the Garbage Classification Dataset, split into training, validation, and testing.

    Args:
        data_dir: The root directory of the dataset, with subdirectories for each class.
        batch_size: Number of samples per batch.
        valid_split: Fraction of the training data to be used for validation.

    Returns:
        train_loader: Iterable DataLoader for training data.
        val_loader: Iterable DataLoader for validation data.
        test_loader: Iterable DataLoader for testing data.
        classes: List of class names.
    """
    # Define transformations
    transform = transforms.Compose([
        transforms.Resize((128, 128)),  # Resize to 128x128
        transforms.ToTensor(),         # Convert to Tensor
        transforms.Normalize((0.5,), (0.5,))  # Normalize to [-1, 1]
    ])

    # Load the full dataset
    dataset = datasets.ImageFolder(data_dir, transform=transform)
    classes = dataset.classes  # Get class names

    # Split the dataset
    total_size = len(dataset)
    test_size = int(0.1 * total_size)  # 10% for testing
    valid_size = int(valid_split * (total_size - test_size))  # Valid from remaining
    train_size = total_size - test_size - valid_size

    train_dataset, val_dataset, test_dataset = random_split(dataset, [train_size, valid_size, test_size])

    # Create DataLoaders
    train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True, num_workers=2)
    val_loader = DataLoader(val_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False, num_workers=2)
    test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False, num_workers=2)

    return train_loader, val_loader, test_loader, classes

###############################################################################
# Training

def get_model_name(name, batch_size, learning_rate, epoch):
    """ Generate a name for the model consisting of all the hyperparameter values

    Args:
        config: Configuration object containing the hyperparameters
    Returns:
        path: A string with the hyperparameter name and value concatenated
    """
    path = "model_{0}_bs{1}_lr{2}_epoch{3}".format(name,
                                                   batch_size,
                                                   learning_rate,
                                                   epoch)
    return path

def normalize_label(labels):
    """
    Normalize labels for multi-class classification.

    Args:
        labels: A 1D tensor of scalar class labels.
    Returns:
        The same labels (already suitable for multi-class classification).
    """
    return labels

def evaluate(net, loader, criterion):
    """ Evaluate the network on a given dataset.

     Args:
         net: PyTorch neural network object.
         loader: PyTorch DataLoader for valuation data.
         criterion: The loss function.

     Returns:
         err: Average classification error rate over the validation set.
         loss: Average loss value over the validation set.
    """
    total_loss = 0.0
    total_err = 0.0
    total_samples = 0
    net = net.to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        for inputs, labels in loader:
            inputs = inputs.to(device)
            labels = labels.to(device)
            outputs = net(inputs)

            # Compute loss
            loss = criterion(outputs, labels)

            # Compute classification error
            _, predicted = torch.max(outputs, 1)
            total_err += (predicted != labels).sum().item()
            total_loss += loss.item()
            total_samples += len(labels)

    err = total_err / total_samples
    avg_loss = total_loss / len(loader)
    return err, avg_loss

###############################################################################
# Training Curve

def plot_training_curve(path, return_val_error=False):
    """ Plot training and validation error/loss curves.

    Args:
        path: Base path for the CSV files containing training logs.
        return_val_error: If True, return the final validation error.
    """
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    import numpy as np

    train_err = np.loadtxt(f"{path}_train_err.csv")
    val_err = np.loadtxt(f"{path}_val_err.csv")
    train_loss = np.loadtxt(f"{path}_train_loss.csv")
    val_loss = np.loadtxt(f"{path}_val_loss.csv")

    if return_val_error:
        return val_err[-1]  # Return the final validation error

    plt.figure()
    plt.title("Train vs Validation Error")
    plt.plot(range(1, len(train_err) + 1), train_err, label="Train")
    plt.plot(range(1, len(val_err) + 1), val_err, label="Validation")
    plt.xlabel("Epoch")
    plt.ylabel("Error")
    plt.legend(loc='best')
    plt.show()

    plt.figure()
    plt.title("Train vs Validation Loss")
    plt.plot(range(1, len(train_loss) + 1), train_loss, label="Train")
    plt.plot(range(1, len(val_loss) + 1), val_loss, label="Validation")
    plt.xlabel("Epoch")
    plt.ylabel("Loss")
    plt.legend(loc='best')
    plt.show()

Part B-1. Visualizing the Data¶

We will make use of the truncated version of garbage classification dataset, which consists of color images of various garbage items such as batteries, biological items, cardboards, and more. These images are resized to 128x128 pixels. You can find the full dataset at https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/sudipp/garbage-dataset-9-classes

Download the "garbage_dataset.zip" file from Quercus and upload it to your colab session storage. Run the provided code to automatically unzip the dataset and split the dataset into training, validation, and testing sets.

In [36]:
!rm -rf /content/garbage_dataset/
_ = !unzip "/content/garbage_dataset.zip"
In [37]:
# Load the Garbage Classification Dataset
data_dir = "./garbage_dataset/"  # Path to the dataset directory
batch_size = 1  # One image per batch for visualization

# Use the get_data_loader function to load the dataset
train_loader, val_loader, test_loader, classes = get_data_loader(
    data_dir=data_dir, batch_size=batch_size
)

Mounted at /content/drive

Part B-1(i) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Below is some sample plotting code that visualizes a small batch of images from your training set. Currently, it plots 15 images in a 3 × 5 grid.

Modify the code so that it instead plots 30 images in a 3 × 10 grid, and displays the ground-truth label above each subplot. You may use the provided code as a starting point.

In [ ]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

k = 0
for images, labels in train_loader:
    # since batch_size = 1, there is only 1 image in `images`
    image = images[0]
    label = labels[0]
    # place the colour channel at the end, instead of at the beginning
    img = np.transpose(image, [1,2,0])
    # normalize pixel intensity values to [0, 1]
    img = img / 2 + 0.5
    plt.subplot(3, 10, k+1)
    plt.axis('off')
    plt.imshow(img)
    plt.title(classes[label], fontsize=8)

    k += 1
    if k > 29:
        break

Part B-1(ii) [1pt EXPLORATORY]¶

How many training examples do we have for the combined classes? What about validation examples? What about test examples?

Plot the distribution of class labels in training/validation/test. Comment on whether the dataset is balanced or imbalanced.

In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

# Count number of samples in each split
train_size = len(train_loader.dataset)
val_size = len(val_loader.dataset)
test_size = len(test_loader.dataset)

print(f"Training examples: {train_size}")
print(f"Validation examples: {val_size}")
print(f"Test examples: {test_size}")
print(f"Total examples: {train_size + val_size + test_size}")


# Analyze class distribution
def get_class_distribution(loader):
    class_counts = {}
    for _, labels in loader:
        label = labels.item()
        class_counts[label] = class_counts.get(label, 0) + 1
    return class_counts

# Get distributions for each split
train_dist = get_class_distribution(train_loader)
val_dist = get_class_distribution(val_loader)
test_dist = get_class_distribution(test_loader)


# Plot class distributions
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))

# Training set distribution
axes[0].bar(range(len(classes)), [train_dist.get(i, 0) for i in range(len(classes))])
axes[0].set_xlabel('Class')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Number of samples')
axes[0].set_title('Training Set Distribution')
axes[0].set_xticks(range(len(classes)))
axes[0].set_xticklabels(classes, rotation=45, ha='right')

# Validation set distribution
axes[1].bar(range(len(classes)), [val_dist.get(i, 0) for i in range(len(classes))])
axes[1].set_xlabel('Class')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Number of samples')
axes[1].set_title('Validation Set Distribution')
axes[1].set_xticks(range(len(classes)))
axes[1].set_xticklabels(classes, rotation=45, ha='right')

# Test set distribution
axes[2].bar(range(len(classes)), [test_dist.get(i, 0) for i in range(len(classes))])
axes[2].set_xlabel('Class')
axes[2].set_ylabel('Number of samples')
axes[2].set_title('Test Set Distribution')
axes[2].set_xticks(range(len(classes)))
axes[2].set_xticklabels(classes, rotation=45, ha='right')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


# Print detailed statistics
print("\nClass distribution:")
print(f"{'Class':<15} {'Train':<10} {'Val':<10} {'Test':<10}")
for i, class_name in enumerate(classes):
    train_count = train_dist.get(i, 0)
    val_count = val_dist.get(i, 0)
    test_count = test_dist.get(i, 0)
    print(f"{class_name:<15} {train_count:<10} {val_count:<10} {test_count:<10}")

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

The training set is well balanced across the 7 classes, with about 204–227 images per class.

The validation and test sets show more variation because they contain fewer samples.

In the validation set, there is moderate imbalance (42 images for glass vs. 67 for paper),
meaning paper has ~60% more samples than glass, which may introduce slight bias in validation metrics.

The test set shows more noticeable imbalance. Plastic has only 19 samples, while battery and biological each have 36.






'''

Part B-2. Training¶

We define two neural networks, a LargeNet and SmallNet. We'll be training the networks in this section.

You won't understand fully what these networks are doing until the next few classes, and that's okay. For this assignment, please focus on learning how to train networks, and how hyperparameters affect training.

In [51]:
class LargeNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(LargeNet, self).__init__()
        self.name = "large"
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(3, 5, 5)
        self.pool = nn.MaxPool2d(2, 2)
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(5, 10, 5)
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(10 * 29 * 29, 32)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(32, 7)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.pool(F.relu(self.conv1(x)))
        x = self.pool(F.relu(self.conv2(x)))
        x = x.view(-1, 10 * 29 * 29)
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = self.fc2(x)
        x = x.squeeze(1) # Flatten to [batch_size]
        return x
In [52]:
class SmallNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(SmallNet, self).__init__()
        self.name = "small"
        self.conv = nn.Conv2d(3, 5, 3)
        self.pool = nn.MaxPool2d(2, 2)
        self.fc = nn.Linear(5 * 31 * 31, 7)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.pool(F.relu(self.conv(x)))
        x = self.pool(x)
        x = x.view(-1, 5 * 31 * 31)
        x = self.fc(x)
        x = x.squeeze(1) # Flatten to [batch_size]
        return x
In [53]:
small_net = SmallNet()
large_net = LargeNet()

Part B-2(i) [1pt EXPLORATORY]¶

The methods small_net.parameters() and large_net.parameters() produces an iterator of all the trainable parameters of the network. These parameters are torch tensors containing many scalar values.

We haven't learned how how the parameters in these high-dimensional tensors will be used, but we should be able to count the number of parameters. Measuring the number of parameters in a network is one way of measuring the "size" of a network.

What is the total number of parameters in small_net and in large_net? (Hint: how many numbers are in each tensor?)

In [ ]:
for param in small_net.parameters():
    print(param.shape)

torch.Size([7, 4805])
torch.Size([7])

    print(param.shape)

In [49]:
# TO BE COMPLETE

# Count total parameters
def count_parameters(model):
    total_params = 0
    for param in model.parameters():
        total_params += param.numel()
    return total_params

small_params = count_parameters(small_net)
large_params = count_parameters(large_net)

print(f"SmallNet total parameters: {small_params:,}")
print(f"LargeNet total parameters: {large_params:,}")

SmallNet total parameters: 33,782
LargeNet total parameters: 271,023
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


 Total parameters from SmallNet are 33,782.

 LargeNet has total parameters of 271,023.



'''

The function train_net¶

The function train_net below takes an untrained neural network (like small_net and large_net) and several other parameters. You should be able to understand how this function works. The figure below shows the high level training loop for a machine learning model:

In [41]:
# Directory in Drive to save everything
save_dir = "/content/drive/MyDrive/garbage_model_runs"
os.makedirs(save_dir, exist_ok=True)
In [42]:
def train_net(net, batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.01, num_epochs=30, save_dir=save_dir):
    # Moving model to GPU
    device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
    print("Using device:", device)
    net = net.to(device)
    ########################################################################
    # Train a classifier on an 7-class classification problem
    target_classes = ["battery", "biological", "cardboard", "glass",
                      "metal", "paper", "plastic"]
    ########################################################################
    # Fixed PyTorch random seed for reproducible results
    torch.manual_seed(1000)
    ########################################################################
    # Obtain the PyTorch data loader objects to load batches of the datasets
    train_loader, val_loader, test_loader, classes = get_data_loader(
            data_dir='/content/garbage_dataset',  # Ensure this is where your dataset is stored
            batch_size=batch_size)

    # Make sure the classes are as expected
    assert len(classes) == 7, "The dataset should have exactly 7 classes."
    ########################################################################
    # Define the Loss function and optimizer
    # Use CrossEntropyLoss for multi-class classification
    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()  # Change to CrossEntropyLoss for multi-class
    optimizer = optim.SGD(net.parameters(), lr=learning_rate, momentum=0.9)
    ########################################################################
    # Set up some numpy arrays to store the training/test loss/accuracy
    train_err = np.zeros(num_epochs)
    train_loss = np.zeros(num_epochs)
    val_err = np.zeros(num_epochs)
    val_loss = np.zeros(num_epochs)
    ########################################################################
    # Train the network
    # Loop over the data iterator and sample a new batch of training data
    # Get the output from the network, and optimize our loss function.
    start_time = time.time()
    for epoch in range(num_epochs):  # Loop over the dataset multiple times
        total_train_loss = 0.0
        total_train_err = 0.0
        total_epoch = 0
        for i, data in enumerate(train_loader, 0):
            # Get the inputs
            inputs, labels = data
            inputs = inputs.to(device)
            labels = labels.to(device)
            # Zero the parameter gradients
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            # Forward pass, backward pass, and optimize
            outputs = net(inputs)
            loss = criterion(outputs, labels)  # No need to normalize labels here for CrossEntropyLoss
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
            # Calculate the statistics
            _, predicted = torch.max(outputs, 1)  # Get the class with the highest probability
            total_train_err += (predicted != labels).sum().item()  # Count errors
            total_train_loss += loss.item()
            total_epoch += len(labels)

        # Calculate error and loss for the training and validation set
        train_err[epoch] = float(total_train_err) / total_epoch
        train_loss[epoch] = float(total_train_loss) / (i + 1)
        val_err[epoch], val_loss[epoch] = evaluate(net, val_loader, criterion)

        # Print stats for the current epoch
        print(f"Epoch {epoch + 1}: Train err: {train_err[epoch]:.4f}, Train loss: {train_loss[epoch]:.4f} | "
              f"Validation err: {val_err[epoch]:.4f}, Validation loss: {val_loss[epoch]:.4f}")

        # Save the current model (checkpoint) to a file
        model_path = os.path.join(
            save_dir,
            get_model_name(net.name, batch_size, learning_rate, epoch)
        )
        torch.save(net.state_dict(), model_path)

    print('Finished Training')
    end_time = time.time()
    elapsed_time = end_time - start_time
    print(f"Total time elapsed: {elapsed_time:.2f} seconds")

    # Write the train/test loss/error into CSV files for plotting later
    epochs = np.arange(1, num_epochs + 1)
    np.savetxt(f"{model_path}_train_err.csv", train_err)
    np.savetxt(f"{model_path}_train_loss.csv", train_loss)
    np.savetxt(f"{model_path}_val_err.csv", val_err)
    np.savetxt(f"{model_path}_val_loss.csv", val_loss)

Part B-2(ii) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

The parameters to the function train_net are hyperparameters of our neural network. We made these hyperparameters easy to modify so that we can tune them later on.

What are the default values of the parameters batch_size, learning_rate, and num_epochs?

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


The default values are:
batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.01, num_epochs=30


'''

Part B-2(iii) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

What files are written to disk when we call train_net with small_net, and train for 5 epochs? Provide a list of all the files written to disk, and what information the files contain.

In [46]:
train_net(small_net, batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.01, num_epochs=5, save_dir=save_dir)

Using device: cpu
Epoch 1: Train err: 0.7183, Train loss: 1.7972 | Validation err: 0.6799, Validation loss: 1.7478
Epoch 2: Train err: 0.5860, Train loss: 1.5100 | Validation err: 0.5370, Validation loss: 1.4774
Epoch 3: Train err: 0.4544, Train loss: 1.2578 | Validation err: 0.5714, Validation loss: 1.5227
Epoch 4: Train err: 0.4319, Train loss: 1.1744 | Validation err: 0.5503, Validation loss: 1.4074
Epoch 5: Train err: 0.3975, Train loss: 1.0815 | Validation err: 0.5079, Validation loss: 1.4041
Finished Training
Total time elapsed: 85.25 seconds
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

When calling train_net(small_net, batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.01) for 5 epochs,
the following files are written:

model_small_bs64_lr0.01_epoch0
model_small_bs64_lr0.01_epoch1
model_small_bs64_lr0.01_epoch2
model_small_bs64_lr0.01_epoch3
model_small_bs64_lr0.01_epoch4

These files contain weights and biases of the neural network like the checkpoint in training.
So if we want to retrive from these particular hyperparameter settings (SmallNet, 5 epochs),
we know weights and biases based on these files.


'''

Part B-2(iv) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Train both small_net and large_net using the function train_net and its default parameters. The function will write many files to disk, including a model checkpoint (saved values of model weights) at the end of each epoch.

If you are using Google Colab, you will need to mount Google Drive so that the files generated by train_net gets saved. We will be using these files in the parts that follow. (See the Google Colab tutorial for more information about this.)

Report the total time elapsed when training each network. Which network took longer to train? Why?

In [ ]:
# Since the function writes files to disk, you will need to mount
# your Google Drive to save model runs later. If you are working on the assignment locally, you
# can comment out this code.

from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

Drive already mounted at /content/drive; to attempt to forcibly remount, call drive.mount("/content/drive", force_remount=True).
In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

# Train SmallNet
print("Training SmallNet...")
small_net = SmallNet()
train_net(small_net)

# Train LargeNet
print("\nTraining LargeNet...")
large_net = LargeNet()
train_net(large_net)

'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

Both nets were run on cuda GPU.

For the SmallNet, the total time elapsed was 253.94 seconds.

For the LargeNet, the total time elapsed was 260.73 seconds.

The LargeNet took longer to train than the SmallNet because the LargeNet has more layers.




'''

Part B-2(v) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Use the function plot_training_curve to display the trajectory of the training/validation error and the training/validation loss. You will need to use the function get_model_name to generate the argument to the plot_training_curve function.

Do this for both the small network and the large network. Include both plots in your writeup.

In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

# Plot training curves for SmallNet
print("SmallNet Training Curves:")
small_model_path = get_model_name("small", batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.01, epoch=29)
small_model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, small_model_path)
plot_training_curve(small_model_path)


# Plot training curves for LargeNet
print("\nLargeNet Training Curves:")
large_model_path = get_model_name("large", batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.01, epoch=29)
large_model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, large_model_path)
plot_training_curve(large_model_path)

SmallNet Training Curves:

LargeNet Training Curves:
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

Both networks show a clear pattern of overfitting.

For SmallNet, the training error decreases rapidly from ~0.73 to nearly 0 by around epochs 18–20 and remains near 0 afterward.
The validation error also drops early (from ~0.65 to ~0.49 by around epoch 5), but then stays relatively stable with small fluctuations.
In terms of loss, the training loss decreases steadily toward 0, while the validation loss shows a brief initial decrease and
then begins to increase after the first few epochs (around epoch 5).
This divergence between training and validation loss indicates overfitting.

For LargeNet, the training and validation error follow a similar overall trend, but the validation error reaches a slightly
lower value than SmallNet (down to ~0.45 around epoch 10). However, the validation loss increases more dramatically than in SmallNet,
reaching ~4.3 by epoch 30. It suggests even stronger overfitting.
This shows that LargeNet has a higher capacity and is easier to remember the training set.




'''

Part B-2(vi) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Describe what you notice about the training curve. How do the curves differ for small_net and large_net? Identify any occurences of underfitting and overfitting.

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

Both networks show clear overall overfitting.

In both cases, training error/loss keeps decreasing toward 0, while validation error and validation loss increase after the first few epochs.
It shows that the model did more on memorization rather than improved generalization.
Compared to SmallNet, LargeNet reaches slightly lower validation error early (best ~0.45 vs ~0.49)
but has a more rapidly increasing validation loss (ending ~4.3 vs ~3.25).
This pattern suggests stronger overfitting due to higher capacity.




'''

Part B-3. Optimization Parameters¶

For this section, we will work with large_net only.

Part B-3(i) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Train large_net with all default parameters, except set learning_rate=0.001. Does the model take longer/shorter to train? Plot the training curve. Describe the effect of lowering the learning rate.

In [ ]:
# Note: When we re-construct the model, we start the training
# with *random weights*. If we omit this code, the values of
# the weights will still be the previously trained values.
large_net = LargeNet()
In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

# Reset the model to start with random weights
large_net = LargeNet()

# Train with lower learning rate
print("Training LargeNet with learning_rate=0.001...")
train_net(large_net, learning_rate=0.001)


# Plot training curves
print("\nLargeNet (lr=0.001) Training Curves:")
model_path = get_model_name("large", batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.001, epoch=29)
model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, model_path)
plot_training_curve(model_path)

Epoch 18: Train err: 0.4028, Train loss: 1.0958 | Validation err: 0.5397, Validation loss: 1.4306
Epoch 19: Train err: 0.3882, Train loss: 1.0794 | Validation err: 0.4947, Validation loss: 1.3675
Epoch 20: Train err: 0.3737, Train loss: 1.0332 | Validation err: 0.5503, Validation loss: 1.4553
Epoch 21: Train err: 0.3591, Train loss: 0.9874 | Validation err: 0.5026, Validation loss: 1.4012
Epoch 22: Train err: 0.3221, Train loss: 0.9181 | Validation err: 0.4868, Validation loss: 1.3895
Epoch 23: Train err: 0.3148, Train loss: 0.9075 | Validation err: 0.5000, Validation loss: 1.4718
Epoch 24: Train err: 0.3280, Train loss: 0.9131 | Validation err: 0.5265, Validation loss: 1.4407
Epoch 25: Train err: 0.2851, Train loss: 0.8494 | Validation err: 0.5026, Validation loss: 1.4683
Epoch 26: Train err: 0.2996, Train loss: 0.8455 | Validation err: 0.5106, Validation loss: 1.4420
Epoch 27: Train err: 0.2639, Train loss: 0.7704 | Validation err: 0.5053, Validation loss: 1.5340
Epoch 28: Train err: 0.2956, Train loss: 0.7952 | Validation err: 0.5265, Validation loss: 1.7168
Epoch 29: Train err: 0.2679, Train loss: 0.7761 | Validation err: 0.5000, Validation loss: 1.4479
Epoch 30: Train err: 0.2143, Train loss: 0.6549 | Validation err: 0.4841, Validation loss: 1.4886
Finished Training
Total time elapsed: 271.12 seconds

LargeNet (lr=0.001) Training Curves:
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

When the learning rate is set to be 0.001, and we keep the model running on cuda GPU.

The training time (271.12 seconds) is longer than before.
The previous condition contained learning_rate as 0.01 and training time as 260.73 seconds.

From this observation, it seems like that the learning rate does not significantly change the amount of computation per epoch,
so the small difference is likely due to run-to-run variation rather than learning rate itself.

When we set smaller learning rate, this can lead to slower convergence. From the graph we can see training error decreases gradually
and remains relatively high at the end, whereas with lr=0.01 the training error approached 0.

Since we see such a pattern, it could also suggest that the model is reducing the overfitting pattern as well.
The validation loss does not explode as in the previous case. It decreases early and then stays around 1.35–1.55 with moderate fluctuations.

Overall, from this experiment, a smaller learning rate makes weight updates more conservative.
It suggests that training is slower and may require more epochs to reach low training error,
but it tends to produce more stable validation loss and reduces severe overfitting.

'''

Part B-3(ii) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Train large_net with all default parameters, except set learning_rate=0.1. Does the model take longer/shorter to train? Plot the training curve. Describe the effect of increasing the learning rate.

In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

# Reset the model to start with random weights
large_net = LargeNet()

# Train with higher learning rate
print("Training LargeNet with learning_rate=0.1")
train_net(large_net, learning_rate=0.1)

# Plot training curves
print("\nLargeNet (lr=0.1) Training Curves:")
model_path = get_model_name("large", batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.1, epoch=29)
model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, model_path)
plot_training_curve(model_path)

LargeNet (lr=0.1) Training Curves:
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

Now, when we set the learning rate to be .1, and we keep the model running on cuda GPU.

The training time (253.12 seconds) is shorter than the previous two conditions .
The previous conditions are lr=.01 with 260.73 seconds & lr=.001 with 271.12 seconds.

When we only comparing the learning rate and the time, it seems like that the larger the learning rate
and faster the training time. But the graphs here (lr=.1) show a clear unstable pattern.

For the first 10 epochs, both train and validation error stay very high (0.85) and the loss barely improves.
They all indicate poor progress.

After epoch 12, the training error drops sharply, but it it unstable, with spikes around epoch ~19–20.
It shows that updates are too large and the optimizer overshoots good solutions.
While validation error remains high (~0.75–0.87) with little consistent improvement, validation loss increases dramatically
and becomes highly volatile, reaching values over 6. This suggests
divergence rather than healthy learning.

Overall, the current condition with learning rate as 0.1 might be too large for the model,
so it leads to poor generalization and unstable training.


'''

Part B-3(iii) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Train large_net with all default parameters, including with learning_rate=0.01. Now, set batch_size=512. Does the model take longer/shorter to train? Plot the training curve. Describe the effect of increasing the batch size.

In [ ]:
# Reset the model to start with random weights
large_net = LargeNet()

# Train with larger batch size
print("Training with batch_size=512")
train_net(large_net, batch_size=512, learning_rate=0.01)

# Plot training curves
print("\nLargeNet (batch_size=512) Training Curves:")
model_path = get_model_name("large", batch_size=512, learning_rate=0.01, epoch=29)
model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, model_path)
plot_training_curve(model_path)

LargeNet (batch_size=512) Training Curves:
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


The current condition has batch_size as 512 and learning_rate as 0.01.
Comparing with the previous condition (lr=0.01. batch_size=64, time=260.73 seconds), the current training time is longer(263.79 seconds).

In the current condition, training becomes smoother and less noisy as we can see from the graph. Both training
and validation curves are much smoother with fewer fluctuations.

Also, the training error decreases much more slowly and remains relatively high (0.38 at epoch 30),
and training loss stays around ~1.02 rather than approaching 0.
Validation loss stays around ~1.33–1.38 instead of increasing sharply. This suggests better generalization.

Overall, a larger batch size gives more stable gradient estimates and reduces severe overfitting,
but it also reduces the number of updates per epoch; therefore, leading to slower improvement in training error/loss.




'''

Part B-3(iv) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Train large_net with all default parameters, including with learning_rate=0.01. Now, set batch_size=16. Does the model take longer/shorter to train? Plot the training curve. Describe the effect of decreasing the batch size.

In [ ]:
# Reset the model to start with random weights
large_net = LargeNet()

# Train with smaller batch size
print("Training with batch_size=16")
train_net(large_net, batch_size=16, learning_rate=0.01)


# Plot training curves
print("\nLargeNet (batch_size=16) Training Curves:")
model_path = get_model_name("large", batch_size=16, learning_rate=0.01, epoch=29)
model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, model_path)
plot_training_curve(model_path)

LargeNet (batch_size=16) Training Curves:
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


The current condition has batch_size as 16 and learning_rate as 0.01.
Comparing with the previous condition (lr=0.01. batch_size=64, time=260.73 seconds),
the current training time is longer(263.61 seconds).

From the graph, the pattern is pretty similar as the all default condition, but there is a faster fitting to the training set.
Training error drops quickly and becomes very low (~0.08 by epoch 30), and training loss decreases to ~0.31.

In terms of validation error, it does not improve much and stays high (~0.60–0.66),
while validation loss increases dramatically and becomes extremely unstable,reaching values around 6–7 by later epochs.


If we comparing the current condition with the previous one condition(batch_size=512, time=263.79 seconds), the current condition has a shorter training time.
The previous one shows smooth curves and small train–val gap), batch_size=16 shows much larger train–val gap and highly unstable validation loss

Overall, decreasing batch size makes training updates noisier and increases overfitting and instability in this experiment.



'''

Part B-4. Hyperparameter Search¶

Part B-4(i) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Based on the plots from above, choose another set of values for the hyperparameters (network, batch_size, learning_rate) that you think would help you improve the validation accuracy. Justify your choice.

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


Based on the previous experiments, I would choose:

- LargeNet,
- batch_size=512,
- learning_rate=0.001.


From the previous experiments, LargeNet has higher capacity than SmallNet and achieves lower training
error, so it is less likely to underfit.

However, with the default hyperparameters (batch_size=64,lr=0.01), the model clearly overfits.
Training error goes to 0 while validation error becomes stable and validation loss increases sharply.

Increasing batch_size to 512 produced a much smaller train–validation gap and a much more stable
validation loss. This can improve generalization. Lowering the learning rate to 0.001 also stabilized training
and reduced the dramatic increase in validation loss seen with lr=0.01.

Therefore, combining these two changes (larger batch + smaller lr) should reduce overfitting
and make optimization more stable, which is likely to improve validation accuracy.


'''

Part B-4(ii) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Train the model with the hyperparameters you chose in part(i), and include the training curve.

In [ ]:

Chosen hyperparameters training curves:

Part B-4(iii) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Based on your result from Part(i), suggest another set of hyperparameter values to try. Justify your choice.

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW

Based on the result (LargeNet, batch_size=512, learning_rate=0.001), both training and validation errors remain high (0.78–0.79)
and are very close to each other across epochs, while the losses decrease slowly.

This suggests the model is not learning enough, likely underfitting.
It could be because the learning rate is too small and the batch size is very large, resulting in very few parameter updates per epoch.


A reasonable next hyperparameter setting:
- LargeNet
- batch_size=256
- learning_rate=0.001


We tried 16 and 512 for batch size from previous experiments.
batch_size=16, lr=0.01 illustrates a very noisy training and poor validation performance, suggesting the model is overfitting.

batch_size=512, lr=0.01 illustrates a relatively well-preformed results, but it also showed a large train–validation gap and rising validation loss (overfitting).

batch_size=512, lr=0.001 shows that the training and validation curves stayed close but with very high error, suggesting the model is underfitting.


Based on the observation, we keep the large-batch stability while using a smaller learning rate and
a slightly smaller batch (256) to improve generalization without slowing learning as much as 512+0.001.


'''

Part B-4(iv) [0.5pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Train the model with the hyperparameters you chose in part(iii), and include the training curve.

In [ ]:

Part B-4(v) [1pt EXPLORATORY]¶

Using the models you trained in earlier subparts of Part B-4, plot a heatmap of validation errors across at least two hyperparameters (e.g., batch size vs. learning rate). Make sure to select at least two values for each hyperparameter and train the model for all combinations. The rows correspond to different batch sizes, the columns correspond to different learning rates, and the cell values are the validation errors.

Briefly explain any trends you observe and what they suggest about the effect of these hyperparameters.

Hint: A heatmap is simply a color-coded grid where each cell’s color represents a numerical value. For example, here’s a small heatmap created with random values:

random_vals = np.random.rand(3, 4)

sns.heatmap(random_vals, annot=True, fmt=".2f")

plt.title("Example Heatmap with Random Values")

plt.show()

In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

# choose the grid
batch_sizes = [64, 256, 512]
learning_rates = [0.001, 0.01]

# store min validation error for each (bs, lr)
val_err_mat = np.zeros((len(batch_sizes), len(learning_rates)))

for i, bs in enumerate(batch_sizes):
    for j, lr in enumerate(learning_rates):
        prefix = get_model_name("large", bs, lr, epoch=29)
        csv_path = os.path.join(save_dir, prefix + "_val_err.csv")

        val_err = np.loadtxt(csv_path)
        val_err_mat[i, j] = val_err.min()  # best checkpoint across epochs

# heatmap
df = pd.DataFrame(
    val_err_mat,
    index=[f"bs={bs}" for bs in batch_sizes],
    columns=[f"lr={lr}" for lr in learning_rates]
)

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
sns.heatmap(df, annot=True, fmt=".3f", vmin=0.43, vmax=0.66)
plt.title("LargeNet: Min Validation Error (batch_size vs learning_rate)")
plt.xlabel("learning_rate")
plt.ylabel("batch_size")
plt.show()
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


From the heatmap, the learning rate has the largest impact on validation performance.

Across all batch sizes, lr=0.01 consistently achieves lower validation error than lr=0.001 (around 0.44–0.46 vs. 0.48–0.78).
This suggests lr=0.001 is too small for a 30-epoch run, and the updates are too conservative,
making training progress slow, and the model tends to underfit within the given training conditions.

Batch size has a secondary effect and interacts with the learning rate. With lr=0.01, a medium batch size performs best.

bs=256 gives the lowest validation error (0.442). This is slightly better than bs=64 (0.447) and bs=512 (0.455).

This likely reflects a trade-off where gradients are stable enough to generalize well without reducing the number of parameter updates too much.

In contrast, when lr=0.001, increasing batch size significantly worsens validation error ( bs=512 reaches 0.778),
because fewer updates per epoch combined with a small step size makes learning even slower.



'''

Part B-5. Evaluating the Best Model¶

Part B-5(i) [2pt MODEL]¶

Summarize in a table the results achieved on training and validation on all your model selections in all parts. This includes choice of small_net vs large_net, the batch_size, learning_rate, and the epoch number. Hint: you can import the Pandas module to create tables.

Then upon reviewing the results choose your best model and load the model checkpoint. You can modify the code below to load your chosen set of weights to the model object net.

In [ ]:
net = LargeNet()
model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, get_model_name(net.name, batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.01, epoch=10))
state = torch.load(model_path)
net.load_state_dict(state)
Out[ ]:
<All keys matched successfully>
In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED


import glob
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

def parse_filename(path):
    # e.g. model_large_bs256_lr0.01_epoch29_val_err.csv
    base = os.path.basename(path)
    parts = base.split("_")
    net = parts[1]                      # small / large
    bs = int(parts[2].replace("bs","")) # 256
    lr = float(parts[3].replace("lr",""))# 0.01
    epoch = int(parts[4].replace("epoch","")) # 29
    return net, bs, lr, epoch

rows = []

# 1) read csv
val_err_files = glob.glob(os.path.join(save_dir, "*_val_err.csv"))

for f in val_err_files:
    net, bs, lr, epoch = parse_filename(f)
    val_err = np.loadtxt(f)          # length = num_epochs
    min_val_err = float(val_err.min())
    best_epoch = int(val_err.argmin())   # 0-based
    rows.append({
        "net": net,
        "batch_size": bs,
        "learning_rate": lr,
        "trained_until_epoch": epoch,
        "min_val_err": min_val_err,
        "best_epoch": best_epoch
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("min_val_err").reset_index(drop=True)
df
Out[ ]:
net batch_size learning_rate trained_until_epoch min_val_err best_epoch
0 large 256 0.010 29 0.441799 22
1 large 64 0.010 29 0.447090 10
2 large 512 0.010 29 0.455026 28
3 small 64 0.010 29 0.470899 4
4 large 64 0.001 29 0.478836 12
5 large 256 0.001 29 0.568783 27
6 large 16 0.010 29 0.592593 4
7 large 64 0.100 29 0.740741 16
8 large 512 0.001 29 0.777778 14
In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

best = df.iloc[0]
print("Best run:", dict(best))

# 2) build the matching network
if best["net"] == "large":
    net = LargeNet()
else:
    net = SmallNet()
net = net.to(device)

# 3) load the checkpoint at best_epoch
model_path = os.path.join(
    save_dir,
    get_model_name(net.name, best["batch_size"], best["learning_rate"], int(best["best_epoch"]))
)

state = torch.load(model_path, map_location=device)
net.load_state_dict(state)
net.eval()

print("Loaded checkpoint:", model_path)

Best run: {'net': 'large', 'batch_size': np.int64(256), 'learning_rate': np.float64(0.01), 'trained_until_epoch': np.int64(29), 'min_val_err': np.float64(0.4417989417989418), 'best_epoch': np.int64(22)}
Loaded checkpoint: /content/drive/MyDrive/garbage_model_runs/model_large_bs256_lr0.01_epoch22

Part B-5(ii) [4pt DISCUSSION]¶

Justify your choice of model from Part (i).

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


Based on the models I trained in all parts, LargeNet with batch_size=256 and learning_rate=0.01 achieves the lowest validation error (0.4418).
This model was better than other tested settings, which showed much higher errors.

This choice is consistent with the trends observed earlier, in which lr=0.01 allows the model to learn efficiently within 30 epochs,
while lr=0.001 is too small and converges very slowly.

A batch size of 256 provides more stable gradient estimates than small batches,
improving generalization.

Finally, LargeNet’s higher capacity makes it better suited for learning more complex visual features than SmallNet.

I selected epoch 22 because it corresponds to the minimum validation error (early-stopping style),
whereas later epochs did not further improve validation performance.




'''

Part B-5(iii) [2pt RESULT]¶

Using the helper code in Part 0, any code from lecture notes, or any code that you write:

  • Compute and report the test classification error for your chosen model.

  • For 5 test images, print the true label of the image, and report the top-3 predicted classes with their probabilities. (“Top-3” means the three classes with the highest predicted probabilities, showing the model’s first, second, and third best guesses.) Comment on whether the probabilities reflect the model’s confidence (e.g., does it give a very high probability to a wrong class, or spread probabilities when uncertain?).

In [ ]:
# If you use the `evaluate` function provided in part 0, you will need to
# set batch_size > 1
train_loader, val_loader, test_loader, classes = get_data_loader(
    data_dir=data_dir,
    batch_size=64)
In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED



# 1) Rebuild the chosen model + load checkpoint
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
net = LargeNet().to(device)

best_bs = 256
best_lr = 0.01
best_epoch = 22  # from "Best run" output

model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, get_model_name("large", batch_size=best_bs, learning_rate=best_lr, epoch=best_epoch))
state = torch.load(model_path, map_location=device)
net.load_state_dict(state)
net.eval()

print("Loaded:", model_path)
print("Using device:", device)

# 2) Load data (batch_size can be anything >1 for evaluation; keep 64 like the template)
train_loader, val_loader, test_loader, classes = get_data_loader(
    data_dir=data_dir,
    batch_size=64
)

# 3) Test classification error
criterion = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
test_err, test_loss = evaluate(net, test_loader, criterion)
print(f"Test error: {test_err:.4f}  |  Test accuracy: {1-test_err:.4f}  |  Test loss: {test_loss:.4f}")

# 4) Show top-3 predictions for 5 test images
def topk_predictions(net, image_tensor, k=3):
    """Return top-k (class_name, prob) for a single image tensor."""
    with torch.no_grad():
        x = image_tensor.unsqueeze(0).to(device)  # [1, C, H, W]
        logits = net(x)
        probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=1).squeeze(0)  # [num_classes]
        top_probs, top_idx = torch.topk(probs, k)
    results = [(classes[i.item()], top_probs[j].item()) for j, i in enumerate(top_idx)]
    return results

# collect 5 samples from test_loader
shown = 0
for inputs, labels in test_loader:
    for j in range(inputs.size(0)):
        img = inputs[j]
        true_idx = labels[j].item()
        true_label = classes[true_idx]

        preds = topk_predictions(net, img, k=3)

        print(f"\nExample {shown+1}")
        print("True label:", true_label)
        print("Top-3 predictions:")
        for rank, (cls_name, p) in enumerate(preds, start=1):
            print(f"  {rank}) {cls_name}: {p:.3f}")

        shown += 1
        if shown >= 5:
            break
    if shown >= 5:
        break

Part B-5(iv) [3pt DISCUSSION]¶

How does the test classification error compare with the validation error? Explain why you would expect the test error to be higher than the validation error.

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


My test classification error is 0.3571 (accuracy 0.6429).
The best validation error for the selected checkpoint was about 0.4418 (validation accuracy about 0.5582).

So in this run, the test error is slightly lower than the validation error.
This means that the model happened to perform better on the test split than on the validation split.

Even though that happened here, in general we would expect test error higher than validation error because:

1. Hyperparameter selection bias. We used the validation set repeatedly to choose hyperparameters and the best epoch.
   But it actually indirectly tunes the model to validation performance.
   This can make the validation estimate optimistically biased.
   Therefore, we assume all the models would have better performance on validation, which means its error smaller than test error.


2. Generalization gap. In our training process, we make the test set as a truly unseen set that is used only once at the end,
   so it better reflects performance on new data. In this case, when we make our decision of model selection based on validation set,
   and we capture the best hyperparameter. That early-stop epoch would interfere our test performance.
   Therefore, we would usually expect more error in the test set.


3. Small sampleing effects. Usually the larger the sample size, the closer the calculated error rate will be to the "true" error rate.
   However, validation (378) and test sets (210) are relatively small, so random differences in class composition
   and difficulty can cause either split to look better or worse by chance.
   Therefore, when test set has a even smaller sample size, the variance of the model's perforance would increase,
   and we would expect test error >= validation error.

DECLARATION: (ChatGPT was used to inspire me about the point of the sampling effects. The justification part was fully writen by myself after I understood this concept.)




'''

Part B-5(v) [3pt DISCUSSION]¶

Why did we only use the test data set at the very end? Why is it important that we use the test data as little as possible?

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


We only used the test set at the very end because it is meant to be an unbiased and as a final evaluation
of how well our model generalizes to truly unseen data.


It’s important to use the test set as little as possible because:

1. Avoid False Success. If the teacher wants to know how well the students learn from the course,
   it would be less likely the teacher shares diretly with the students the final exam questions,
   becasue it is not really testing students' learning performance, but memorlization.
   The same idea applies to test set.

2. Avoid false confidence. If the test set leaks, the model might look perfect on paper with low test error,
   since we are tuning the model based on the test set. However, the model would fail terribly in an actual prediction task.
   This is because it learned/memorized the surface pattern of the test set instead of the underlying patterns.


3. Maintaining statistical integrity. Every time we use the test set to make a decision, we lose its ability to provide
   an unbiased estimate. However, if we use it only once at the end, this action ensures that
   our final report is statistically reasonable and is trustworthy in its reflection of the model’s quality.



'''

Part B-5(vi) [3pt RESULT]¶

Preprocess the images from the secret test set provided below and classify these images into the 7 class labels from the garbage dataset using your chosen model. Report the model predictions by your chosen model on this test dataset as a csv file called 'labels_part_b5.csv', containing the name of the images and their corresponsing predicted labels for all the images in the folder.

import wget
# Correct URL for raw file content on GitHub
raw_url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Sabaae/Dataset/main/secret_testset.zip'
myfile = wget.download(raw_url)
In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

import os
import shutil
import glob
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torchvision import transforms
from PIL import Image
import csv
from google.colab import drive

# Re-defining classes as the kernel was reset
classes = ['battery', 'biological', 'cardboard', 'glass', 'metal', 'paper', 'plastic']

# Mount Drive first to ensure we can access the saved weights
drive.mount('/content/gdrive', force_remount=True)

# Prepare model for inference and load weights
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
net = LargeNet().to(device)

# Set the correct path to the best epoch checkpoint
checkpoint_path = '/content/gdrive/MyDrive/garbage_model_runs/model_large_bs256_lr0.01_epoch22'

if os.path.exists(checkpoint_path):
    print(f"Found it. Loading weights from {checkpoint_path}")
    net.load_state_dict(torch.load(checkpoint_path, map_location=device))
    net.eval()
else:
    # If the above fails, let's list the directory to see where it is
    print(f"Still can't find the file. Current content of MyDrive:")
    !ls /content/gdrive/MyDrive/
    raise FileNotFoundError(f"Checkpoint not found at {checkpoint_path}.")

# Handle the secret test set zip file
zip_file_path = 'secret_testset.zip'
extract_base_dir = '/content/secret_testset_b5/'
os.makedirs(extract_base_dir, exist_ok=True)

print(f"Unzipping {zip_file_path} to {extract_base_dir}")
_ = !unzip -o "{zip_file_path}" -d "{extract_base_dir}"

if os.path.exists(os.path.join(extract_base_dir, '__MACOSX')):
    shutil.rmtree(os.path.join(extract_base_dir, '__MACOSX'))

# Locate the actual image folder and list files
found_image_paths = []
for root, _, files in os.walk(extract_base_dir):
    for file in files:
        if file.lower().endswith(('.jpg', '.jpeg', '.png', '.bmp')):
            found_image_paths.append(os.path.join(root, file))

if not found_image_paths:
    raise FileNotFoundError(f"No image files found in {extract_base_dir}.")

test_dir = os.path.dirname(found_image_paths[0])
image_files = sorted([os.path.basename(p) for p in found_image_paths])

# Transformations match the training phase
transform = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.Resize((128, 128)),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize((0.5,), (0.5,))
])

predictions = []
print(f"Starting inference on {len(image_files)} secret images...")

for img_file in image_files:
    img_path = os.path.join(test_dir, img_file)
    try:
        img = Image.open(img_path).convert('RGB')
        img_tensor = transform(img).unsqueeze(0).to(device)

        with torch.no_grad():
            output = net(img_tensor)
            _, pred = torch.max(output, 1)
            predictions.append(classes[pred.item()])
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error processing image {img_file}: {e}")
        predictions.append('unknown')

# Save the model predictions to the required CSV format
output_csv_filename = 'labels_part_b5.csv'
with open(output_csv_filename, 'w', newline='') as f:
    writer = csv.writer(f)
    writer.writerow(['image_name', 'predicted_label'])
    for name, pred in zip(image_files, predictions):
        writer.writerow([name, pred])

print(f"\nPredictions saved to {output_csv_filename} ({len(predictions)} images)")

Mounted at /content/gdrive
Found it! Loading weights from /content/gdrive/MyDrive/garbage_model_runs/model_large_bs256_lr0.01_epoch22
Unzipping secret_testset.zip to /content/secret_testset_b5/
Starting inference on 260 secret images...

Predictions saved to labels_part_b5.csv (260 images)

Part B-6. Fully-Connected Linear ANN vs CNN [4pt RESULT]¶

Test out a 3-layer linear fully-connected ANN architecture (see simpleANN below). You should explore different hyperparameter settings to determine how well you can do on the validation dataset. Once satisified with the performance, you may test it out on the test data.

Similar to what you did in the previous part, report the model predictions by your best ANN architecture on the secret test dataset as a csv file called 'labels_part_b6.csv', containing the names of the images and their corresopnding predicted labels for all the images in the folder.

How does the your best CNN model compare with an 2-layer linear ANN model (no convolutional layers) on classifying garbage dataset images?

In [8]:
class simpleANN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(simpleANN, self).__init__()
        self.name = "simple"
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(128*128*3, 256)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(256, 128)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(128, 7)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x.view(-1, 128*128*3)
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = F.relu(self.fc2(x))
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x
In [ ]:

# Train SimpleANN with default parameters

simple_net = simpleANN()
train_net(simple_net, batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.01, num_epochs=30)

# Plot training curves

print("\nSimpleANN Training Curves:")
model_path = get_model_name("simple", batch_size=64, learning_rate=0.01, epoch=29)
model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, model_path)
plot_training_curve(model_path)

# TO BE COMPLETED



import pandas as pd

# 1) Train all hyperparameter combinations
learning_rates = [0.001, 0.01, 0.1]
batch_sizes = [16, 64, 256, 512]
results = []

for lr in learning_rates:
    for bs in batch_sizes:
        print(f"\nTraining: bs={bs}, lr={lr}")

        simple_net = simpleANN()
        train_net(simple_net, batch_size=bs, learning_rate=lr, num_epochs=30)

        device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
        simple_net = simple_net.to(device)
        criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

        _, val_loader, _, _ = get_data_loader(data_dir='/content/garbage_dataset', batch_size=bs)
        val_err, val_loss = evaluate(simple_net, val_loader, criterion)

        results.append({'Batch Size': bs, 'Learning Rate': lr, 'Val Error': val_err})
        print(f"Val Error: {val_err:.4f}")


# 2) Print the Top 5 configurations

df = pd.DataFrame(results)
df_top5 = df.sort_values('Val Error').head(5)
print("\nTop 5 Configurations:")
print(df_top5.to_string(index=False))

# TO BE COMPLETED



# 3) Test with best model

best = df.loc[df['Val Error'].idxmin()]
best_bs = int(best['Batch Size'])
best_lr = best['Learning Rate']

net = simpleANN().to(device)
model_path = os.path.join(save_dir, get_model_name("simple", batch_size=best_bs, learning_rate=best_lr, epoch=29))
net.load_state_dict(torch.load(model_path, map_location=device))
net.eval()

_, _, test_loader, classes = get_data_loader(data_dir='/content/garbage_dataset', batch_size=64)
test_err, test_loss = evaluate(net, test_loader, criterion)

print(f"\nBest config: bs={best_bs}, lr={best_lr}")
print(f"Test Error: {test_err:.4f}, Accuracy: {(1-test_err)*100:.2f}%")


# 4) Print the Top-3 predictions for 5 test images

shown = 0
for inputs, labels in test_loader:
    for j in range(inputs.size(0)):
        with torch.no_grad():
            logits = net(inputs[j].unsqueeze(0).to(device))
            probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=1).squeeze()
            top3_p, top3_i = torch.topk(probs, 3)

        print(f"\nImage {shown+1}: True={classes[labels[j]]}")
        for k in range(3):
            print(f"  {k+1}) {classes[top3_i[k]]}: {top3_p[k]:.3f}")

        shown += 1
        if shown >= 5:
            break
    if shown >= 5:
        break

Best config: bs=16, lr=0.001
Test Error: 0.1381, Accuracy: 86.19%

In [16]:
# TO BE COMPLETED

# Predict on secret test set with best SimpleANN

!unzip secret_testset.zip -x "__MACOSX/*" "*/._*" -d secret_test/

from torchvision import transforms
from PIL import Image

transform = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.Resize((128, 128)),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize((0.5,), (0.5,))
])

test_dir = 'secret_test/secret_testset/'
if not os.path.exists(test_dir):
    print("Warning: Path not found! Trying alternative...")
    test_dir = 'secret_test/'
image_files = sorted([f for f in os.listdir(test_dir) if f.endswith(('.jpg', '.png'))])

net.eval()
predictions = []
for img_file in image_files:
    img = Image.open(os.path.join(test_dir, img_file)).convert('RGB')
    img_tensor = transform(img).unsqueeze(0).to(device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        output = net(img_tensor)
        _, pred = torch.max(output, 1)
        predictions.append(classes[pred.item()])

import csv
with open('labels_part_b6.csv', 'w', newline='') as f:
    writer = csv.writer(f)
    writer.writerow(['image_name', 'predicted_label'])
    for name, pred in zip(image_files, predictions):
        writer.writerow([name, pred])

print(f"Saved labels_part_b6.csv ({len(predictions)} images)")

In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


Q:How does the your best CNN model compare with an 2-layer linear ANN model (no convolutional layers) on classifying garbage dataset images?

1. The ANN model was trained with different hyperparameter settings and selected the one (bs=16,lr=0.001) with the lowest validation error(0.1455).
   Then, it was tested on the test set and achieved an accuracy of 86.19%, test error of 0.1381.
   The model demonstrated high confidence in its predictions on the 5 sample test images. Image-3 (Glass) and Image-4 (Battery),
   with probabilities exceeding 98%.
   Image-2 (Plastic) showed slightly more uncertainty with 70.2% confidence.


2. The previous CNN model with LargeNet was trained with batch size of 256, learning rate of 0.01 as the best model.
   The validation error of this model was 0.4418.
   Comparing the CNN and ANN model, we can see the ANN showed a lower validation error than CNN.
   Similarly, ANN achieved higher test accuracy(86.19%) than CNN(64.29%).
   Therefore, ANN has better performance than CNN on this particular dataset.


3. The current ANN model shows an overfitting pattern, its best model has training error nearly 0 (0.0066),
   indicating it has completely memorized the training dataset. However, when evaluated on unseen data,
   the model's error rose to 0.1381 (Test) and 0.1455 (Validation). While these numbers suggest high accuracy (86%),
   there is a distinct generalization gap compared to the near-perfect training score.


4. This difference represents a quantitative measure of overfitting. The ANN could possiblily memorized the pixel values of the limited training set.
   The ANN is likely identifying particular textures or features that are common in both the training and test sets,
   rather than invariant features.


5. Within the current architecture and hyperparameter search range, the CNN did not learn features that generalize as well as the ANN.
   A possible reason is that the dataset size (only a few thousand images) may limit CNN generalization without stronger regularization or data augmentation.
   In addition, the current LargeNet does not use components such as batch normalization or residual connections, which often help optimization
   and generalization in CNNs.
   Overall, ANN might be better suited for the small dataset and more likely to memorize the training data, helping the model generalize better.




'''

PART C (Optional) - Bonus Challenge!¶

This is an optional exercise for those that finish the assignment early and would like to perform a deeper exploration of the assignment.

In part A we constructed and trained a 2-layer neural network from scratch. In Part B we saw how PyTorch can be used to simplify the construction of neural networks by taking care of all the complexity related to gradient calculations, training on GPUs, and structuring your code.

For this bonus challenge we will propose additional task that will have you work towards completing the pipeline and deploying the models online, while exploring ways to improve these models along the way.

Tasks:

  1. Modify your code to classify images that are not "paper" or "cardboard" into the category "other".
  2. Preprocess images from the internet to be able to classify any images into garbage labels such as battery, biological, etc. (i.e., resize, aspect ratio, etc.). Evaluate your best model's performance on images loaded from the internet.
  3. Deploy your best model onto huggingface spaces (or other web hosting services) to classify images into the different garbage classes.

Bonus marks will be provided based on the number of tasks completed and how well they are completed. Summarize below your results and anything intersting you learned from the steps that you completed. Bonus marks cannot be accumulated beyond a maximum assignment grade.

In [ ]:
# TO BE COMPLETED


#  Task1

from collections import defaultdict
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# check device
device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print("Using device:", device)

net = net.to(device)
net.eval()

#Label mapping: 7 to 3
def map_to_paper_cardboard_other(label):
    return label if label in ("paper", "cardboard") else "other"

def collapse_probs_to_3class(top_labels, top_probs):
    agg = defaultdict(float)
    for lbl, p in zip(top_labels, top_probs):
        agg[map_to_paper_cardboard_other(lbl)] += float(p)
    return sorted(agg.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)


#Single image prediction (top-k + mapped)
def predict_single_3class(net, img_tensor, classes, device, topk=3):
    net.eval()
    x = img_tensor.unsqueeze(0).to(device)

    topk = min(topk, len(classes))

    with torch.no_grad():
        probs = F.softmax(net(x), dim=1).squeeze(0)
        top_probs, top_idx = torch.topk(probs, k=topk)

    top_probs = top_probs.cpu().tolist()
    top_labels = [classes[i] for i in top_idx.cpu().tolist()]
    topk_7 = list(zip(top_labels, top_probs))

    topk_3 = collapse_probs_to_3class(top_labels, top_probs)
    final_3 = topk_3[0][0] if topk_3 else "other"
    return topk_7, topk_3, final_3

#Evaluate 3-class accuracy
def evaluate_3class_accuracy(net, loader, classes, device):
    net.eval()
    total, correct = 0, 0

    with torch.no_grad():
        for images, labels in loader:
            images = images.to(device)
            logits = net(images)
            pred_idx = logits.argmax(dim=1).cpu().tolist()
            true_idx = labels.cpu().tolist()

            for p, y in zip(pred_idx, true_idx):
                pred3 = map_to_paper_cardboard_other(classes[p])
                true3 = map_to_paper_cardboard_other(classes[y])
                correct += int(pred3 == true3)
                total += 1

    return correct / total if total else 0.0


#use one image from val_loader so img_tensor is defined
images, labels = next(iter(val_loader))     # take first batch from validation set
img_tensor = images[0]                      # first image tensor [3,H,W]
true_label7 = classes[int(labels[0].item())]
true_label3 = map_to_paper_cardboard_other(true_label7)

top7, top3, final3 = predict_single_3class(net, img_tensor, classes, device, topk=3)

print("\nExample from val_loader:")
print("True label (7-class):", true_label7)
print("True label (3-class):", true_label3)
print("Top-3 (7-class):")
for i, (lbl, p) in enumerate(top7, 1):
    print(f"  {i}) {lbl}: {p:.3f}")

print("Collapsed (3-class):")
for i, (lbl, p) in enumerate(top3, 1):
    print(f"  {i}) {lbl}: {p:.3f}")

print("Final 3-class prediction:", final3)

Using device: cuda

Example from val_loader:
True label (7-class): cardboard
True label (3-class): cardboard
Top-3 (7-class):
  1) biological: 0.418
  2) cardboard: 0.255
  3) paper: 0.141
Collapsed (3-class):
  1) other: 0.418
  2) cardboard: 0.255
  3) paper: 0.141
Final 3-class prediction: other

3-class accuracy | val=0.7646 test=0.7619
In [ ]:
'''
PROVIDE YOUR ANSWER BELOW


For Task 1, I kept the original 7-class CNN unchanged and implemented an output-layer mapping to collapse predictions into three categories: paper, cardboard, and other.
Specifically, if the predicted class is “paper” or “cardboard” I keep it; otherwise I map it to “other”.

This mapping is useful when the application only needs to distinguish recyclable paper/cardboard from all other garbage types.
However, it does not “fix” misclassifications — it only changes how predictions are interpreted.
For example, one validation image with true label cardboard had top-1 prediction biological (0.418) and top-2 cardboard (0.255).
After mapping, the model’s final prediction becomes other because biological belongs to the “other” group.

After applying this mapping, the 3-class accuracy improved to 0.7646 on the validation set and 0.7619 on the test set,
which is higher than the original 7-class setting because many errors between non-paper categories are no longer counted as mistakes when they are grouped into “other”.



'''

Saving to HTML¶

Detailed instructions for saving to HTML can be found here. Provided below are a summary of the instructions:

(1) download your ipynb file by clicking on File->Download.ipynb

(2) reupload your file to the temporary Google Colab storage (you can access the temporary storage from the tab to the left)

(3) run the following:

In [ ]:
%%shell
jupyter nbconvert --to html A1.ipynb

[NbConvertApp] Converting notebook A1.ipynb to html
[NbConvertApp] Writing 433116 bytes to A1.html
Out[ ]:
























(4) the html file will be available for download in the temporary Google Colab storage


(5) review the html file and make sure all the results are visible before submitting your assignment to Quercus






















Assignment Grading Rubric¶
The grading of the assignment will be based on the following categories:


(1) 10 Pt - EXPLORATORY QUESTIONS These are basic questions that in most cases can be answered without requiring a fully working and trained neural network model. For example, data loading, processing and visualization, summary statistics, data exploration, model and training setup, etc.


(2) 10 Pt - MODEL Student has successfully implemented all the required neural network models and has demonstrated successful training of the model without any errors.


(3) 10 Pt - RESULT Students are evaluated based on the results achieved in comparison to the expected results of the assignment.


(4) 10 Pt - DISCUSSION QUESTIONS Student demonstrated understanding beyond the basic exploratory questions, can answer some of the more challenging questions, and provide arguments for their model selection decisions.


(5) 10 Pt - COMMUNICATION Student has provided a quality submission that is easy to read without too many unnecessary output statements that distract the reading of the document. The code has been well commented and all the answers are communicated clearly and concisely.


(6) 5 Pt - BONUS Student has completed the assignment and has taken on the challenging bonus tasks listed in PART C. The student has demonstrated a good understanding of all aspects of the assignment and has exceeded expectations for the assignment.


TOTAL GRADE = _____ of 50 Pts
